name: Keycloak
what_it_does: Open-source Identity and Access Management server providing SSO, OAuth2/OIDC, and SAML — self-hosted or via Red Hat SSO managed offering.
use_cases: Stand up a fully self-hosted OIDC provider with no external dependencies for air-gapped or compliance-sensitive environments
Federate multiple identity sources (LDAP, Active Directory, social providers) into a single unified token-issuing authority
Implement fine-grained authorization using Keycloak's Authorization Services (UMA 2.0, RBAC, ABAC) for complex multi-tenant applications
Automate realm and client configuration via the Admin REST API for GitOps-driven infrastructure provisioning
Migrate users from a legacy identity system into Keycloak with password hash import, keeping users unaware of the migration
not_for: Teams without ops capacity to manage JVM-based infrastructure, upgrades, backups, and HA clustering
Projects needing a fully managed SaaS identity service with zero infrastructure overhead
Simple API key or service account auth where a lightweight solution would suffice
best_when: You need full control over your identity infrastructure, have no external network restrictions, and can invest in operational management of a self-hosted service.
avoid_when: Your team lacks the capacity to operate and maintain a JVM-based clustered service, or you need enterprise SLA guarantees without managing infrastructure.

auth.notes: Self-hosted — auth is configured by the operator. Admin REST API uses bearer tokens obtained via client credentials or admin-cli. No external auth dependency; all token issuance is local.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Completely free; open-source under Apache 2.0 license
pricing.paid_tiers: Red Hat SSO (managed/supported version) available with enterprise subscription; cloud-hosted options via third-party vendors
pricing.notes: Core product is free. Red Hat subscription provides enterprise support, patches, and a managed offering. Self-hosting costs are infrastructure only.

performance.rate_limits: No built-in rate limits by default; operators must configure brute-force protection and reverse proxy rate limiting. Performance scales with JVM heap and cluster size.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Admin API errors vary by endpoint — some return structured JSON with error and error_description, others return HTML or empty 400s. OAuth2 error responses follow RFC 6749.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: User and realm creation endpoints return 409 Conflict for duplicates — agents should catch and treat as success. Role assignment and group membership calls are idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: Admin REST API paths changed significantly between major versions (v18, v20, v22+) — agents must pin to a specific version or handle 404s gracefully
Obtaining an admin token requires a separate POST to /realms/master/protocol/openid-connect/token — agents must not confuse realm admin tokens with application user tokens
Realm import via REST API silently ignores unknown fields; failed partial imports can leave realms in inconsistent state without a clear error
Session invalidation (logout) requires posting to the end_session endpoint with the ID token hint — calling DELETE on the session resource in the admin API affects different objects

Offline tokens and refresh tokens have separate expiry policies configurable per realm — agents relying on long-lived refresh tokens must verify realm policy or handle unexpected 400 invalid_grant errors

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS configuration is operator responsibility — must be enforced at proxy or server level. Supports HSM for key storage. Brute-force protection and CORS must be configured explicitly.
